one_liner: A 34-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of itchy, scaly red plaques on his elbows, knees, and scalp that flared after he scraped his forearm on a garden fence.

stage2.hpi_extension: He first noticed small scaly patches on his elbows about two years ago but they have enlarged and spread. New lesions appeared in a linear streak along a recent scratch on his left forearm. He also describes morning stiffness and a dull ache in his right DIP joints. His fingernails have become pitted and discolored. He denies fevers, weight loss, or mucosal lesions.
stage2.family_history: Father has 'bad dandruff and scaly elbows'; paternal uncle with arthritis.
stage2.social_history: Landscaper. Smokes half a pack/day. Drinks 3-4 beers on weekends. Reports stress from work.
stage2.physical_exam: Skin: Multiple well-demarcated salmon-pink plaques with overlying thick silvery scale symmetrically distributed on the extensor surfaces of both elbows and knees, and within the scalp at the hairline. Gentle removal of scale produces pinpoint bleeding (Auspitz sign). A linear plaque follows a recent scratch on the left forearm (Koebner phenomenon). Nails: pitting, yellow-brown oil spots, and distal onycholysis of several fingernails. Joints: mild tenderness and swelling of the right 2nd and 3rd distal interphalangeal joints without deformity.

lab_results.result: Punch biopsy of an elbow plaque shows marked acanthosis with elongated rete ridges, parakeratosis (retained nuclei in the stratum corneum), thinning of the granular layer, and neutrophilic microabscesses within the stratum corneum (Munro microabscesses). Dilated dermal capillaries are noted in the papillae.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Histology is classic for psoriasis. Parakeratosis and Munro microabscesses reflect the accelerated keratinocyte turnover and neutrophilic infiltration characteristic of the disease.

lab_results.result: ESR mildly elevated at 32 mm/hr. CRP mildly elevated. Uric acid normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Low-grade systemic inflammation is common in extensive psoriasis and in psoriatic arthritis; it also screens for alternative inflammatory arthropathies such as gout.

lab_results.result: Rheumatoid factor negative. Anti-CCP antibodies negative. ANA negative. HIV negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Negative RF and anti-CCP argue against rheumatoid arthritis and support a seronegative spondyloarthropathy pattern consistent with psoriatic arthritis.

stage5.final_clue: He is started on topical high-potency corticosteroids plus a vitamin D analog (calcipotriene) with improvement in his plaques. Because of joint involvement, methotrexate is added. He is counseled that approximately 20% of patients with psoriasis develop psoriatic arthritis.

debrief.classic_presentation: Psoriasis is a chronic, relapsing, immune-mediated skin disease that typically presents with well-demarcated salmon-pink plaques covered by silvery scale on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), the scalp, and the lumbosacral region. Classic signs include the Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale is removed), the Koebner phenomenon (new plaques at sites of skin trauma), and nail pitting with oil spots and onycholysis. Approximately 20% of patients develop psoriatic arthritis, which often targets the DIP joints.
debrief.pathophysiology: Psoriasis is driven by a dysregulated adaptive immune response in which Th1 and Th17 T cells release cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-17, IL-22, IL-23) that stimulate keratinocyte hyperproliferation and turn over the epidermis in days instead of weeks. Histology shows acanthosis, parakeratosis, loss of the granular layer, and Munro microabscesses (neutrophils in the stratum corneum). There is a strong HLA-C (HLA-Cw6) association. Triggers include trauma (Koebner), infection (especially streptococcal, which triggers guttate psoriasis), stress, beta-blockers, lithium, and alcohol.
debrief.treatment_overview: Limited disease: topical corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs (calcipotriene), tazarotene, tar, and calcineurin inhibitors. Moderate-to-severe disease: phototherapy (narrow-band UVB, PUVA), methotrexate, cyclosporine, acitretin, and biologics targeting TNF-alpha (etanercept, infliximab, adalimumab), IL-17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab), and IL-12/23 (ustekinumab). Psoriatic arthritis responds to methotrexate and anti-TNF or anti-IL-17 biologics.
high_yield_pearls: Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding after scale removal) and the Koebner phenomenon (lesions at sites of skin trauma) are classic for psoriasis.
Histology: acanthosis, parakeratosis, Munro microabscesses (neutrophils in stratum corneum), and loss of the granular layer.
Nail findings: pitting, oil spots, and onycholysis — highly associated with psoriatic arthritis.
About 20% of patients with psoriasis develop psoriatic arthritis, which classically involves the DIP joints and can produce a 'pencil-in-cup' deformity.
Psoriasis is driven by Th17 / IL-17 / IL-23 signaling — the rationale for biologics like secukinumab and ustekinumab.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Common skin disorders — Psoriasis' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Seronegative spondyloarthropathies — Psoriatic arthritis' section.
